Real Analysis

The Bolzano–Weierstrass Theorem and Limit Superior/Inferior

The Bolzano–Weierstrass Theorem and Limit Superior/Inferior

The Bolzano–Weierstrass Theorem

Lecture 9 showed that convergent sequences pass every property down to their subsequences, and that unbounded sequences necessarily diverge. A natural question remains: does every bounded sequence — even one that itself diverges, like ((−1)n)((-1)^n)((−1)n) — still contain some convergent subsequence hiding inside it? The Bolzano–Weierstrass theorem answers yes, using the Nested Intervals Property from Lecture 6 as its engine.

Bolzano–Weierstrass Theorem

Every bounded sequence in R\mathbb{R}R has a convergent subsequence.

The strategy is a repeated bisection: trap the sequence in an interval, then keep halving that interval, always keeping the half that still contains infinitely many terms of the sequence. This produces exactly the nested, shrinking intervals that Lecture 6 showed must collapse to a single point.

Proof (Bisection Argument)

Let (xn)(x_n)(xn​) be a bounded sequence. Since it is bounded, there is a closed interval I1=[a,b]I_1 = [a,b]I1​=[a,b] with xn∈I1x_n \in I_1xn​∈I1​ for all nnn. Set n1=1n_1 = 1n1​=1.

Bisect I1I_1I1​ into two equal closed subintervals. Since I1I_1I1​ contains infinitely many terms of (xn)(x_n)(xn​) but is split into only two pieces, at least one piece must itself contain infinitely many terms — call it I2I_2I2​. Choose n2>n1n_2 > n_1n2​>n1​ to be the smallest index with xn2∈I2x_{n_2} \in I_2xn2​​∈I2​.

Repeat: bisect I2I_2I2​, let I3I_3I3​ be whichever half still contains infinitely many terms, and choose n3>n2n_3 > n_2n3​>n2​ minimal with xn3∈I3x_{n_3} \in I_3xn3​​∈I3​. Continuing inductively produces:

  • a nested sequence of closed, bounded intervals I1⊃I2⊃⋯I_1 \supset I_2 \supset \cdotsI1​⊃I2​⊃⋯ with lengths ∣Ik∣=b−a2k−1→0|I_k| = \dfrac{b-a}{2^{k-1}} \to 0∣Ik​∣=2k−1b−a​→0,
  • a strictly increasing sequence of indices (nk)(n_k)(nk​) with xnk∈Ikx_{n_k} \in I_kxnk​​∈Ik​ for each kkk.

By the Nested Intervals Property (Lecture 6), together with the vanishing-length corollary, there exists a unique point ξ∈⋂k=1∞Ik\xi \in \bigcap_{k=1}^\infty I_kξ∈⋂k=1∞​Ik​.

Since both xnkx_{n_k}xnk​​ and ξ\xiξ lie in IkI_kIk​,

∣xnk−ξ∣≤∣Ik∣=b−a2k−1→k→∞0,|x_{n_k} - \xi| \le |I_k| = \frac{b-a}{2^{k-1}} \xrightarrow[k\to\infty]{} 0,∣xnk​​−ξ∣≤∣Ik​∣=2k−1b−a​k→∞​0,

so xnk→ξx_{n_k} \to \xixnk​​→ξ. Thus (xnk)(x_{n_k})(xnk​​) is a convergent subsequence of (xn)(x_n)(xn​).

The proof relies on a specialized version of the Nested Intervals Property, isolated here for reference — it’s the vanishing-length corollary from Lecture 6, restated in terms of interval length rather than sup⁡A\sup AsupA/inf⁡B\inf BinfB.

Nested Interval Theorem (Specialized)

If (Ik)(I_k)(Ik​) is a nested sequence of nonempty closed intervals with ∣Ik∣→0|I_k| \to 0∣Ik​∣→0, then ⋂k=1∞Ik={ξ}\bigcap_{k=1}^\infty I_k = \{\xi\}⋂k=1∞​Ik​={ξ} consists of exactly one point.

The “pigeonhole” intuition

Picture infinitely many pigeons (sequence terms) inside a fenced yard (a bounded interval). Cut the yard in half — at least one half must still contain infinitely many pigeons, by pigeonhole. Keep halving whichever half stays infinitely populated. The fences close in on a single spot, and picking one pigeon from each shrinking yard produces a subsequence converging to that spot.

Limit Superior and Limit Inferior

Bolzano–Weierstrass guarantees some convergent subsequence exists, but a bounded, divergent sequence like ((−1)n)((-1)^n)((−1)n) can have many subsequential limits. Limsup and liminf are designed to capture the largest and smallest of these possible limits — and, as the convergence criterion below shows, comparing the two gives a complete test for convergence itself.

Definitions

Eventual Bounds and limsup/liminf

Let X=(xn)X = (x_n)X=(xn​) be a real sequence.

Define the set of eventual upper bounds

V={v∈R:xn≤v for all but finitely many n}.V = \{v \in \mathbb{R} : x_n \le v \text{ for all but finitely many } n\}.V={v∈R:xn​≤v for all but finitely many n}.

The limit superior is

lim⁡‾ xn=lim sup⁡n→∞xn=inf⁡V.\overline{\lim}\,x_n = \limsup_{n\to\infty} x_n = \inf V.limxn​=n→∞limsup​xn​=infV.

Define the set of eventual lower bounds

W={w∈R:xn≥w for all but finitely many n}.W = \{w \in \mathbb{R} : x_n \ge w \text{ for all but finitely many } n\}.W={w∈R:xn​≥w for all but finitely many n}.

The limit inferior is

lim⁡‾ xn=lim inf⁡n→∞xn=sup⁡W.\underline{\lim}\,x_n = \liminf_{n\to\infty} x_n = \sup W.lim​xn​=n→∞liminf​xn​=supW.

Working directly with VVV and WWW is often awkward. An equivalent, more computable description comes from tracking the running supremum and infimum of the tail of the sequence — quantities that behave monotonically and so converge by the Monotone Convergence Theorem (Lecture 8).

Tail Sup/Inf Characterization

For any real sequence (xn)(x_n)(xn​), define

sn=sup⁡k≥nxk,in=inf⁡k≥nxk.s_n = \sup_{k\ge n} x_k, \qquad i_n = \inf_{k\ge n} x_k.sn​=k≥nsup​xk​,in​=k≥ninf​xk​.

Then (sn)(s_n)(sn​) is nonincreasing and (in)(i_n)(in​) is nondecreasing, and

lim sup⁡n→∞xn=inf⁡n∈Nsn=lim⁡n→∞sn,lim inf⁡n→∞xn=sup⁡n∈Nin=lim⁡n→∞in,\limsup_{n\to\infty} x_n = \inf_{n\in\mathbb{N}} s_n = \lim_{n\to\infty} s_n, \qquad \liminf_{n\to\infty} x_n = \sup_{n\in\mathbb{N}} i_n = \lim_{n\to\infty} i_n,n→∞limsup​xn​=n∈Ninf​sn​=n→∞lim​sn​,n→∞liminf​xn​=n∈Nsup​in​=n→∞lim​in​,

with limits possibly in the extended reals. If (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is bounded, these limits are finite.

Why (sn)(s_n)(sn​) is monotone

Taking the supremum over a shrinking tail {xk:k≥n}\{x_k : k \ge n\}{xk​:k≥n} as nnn increases can only decrease (or maintain) the supremum — hence (sn)(s_n)(sn​) is nonincreasing, and dually (in)(i_n)(in​) is nondecreasing. Since a nonincreasing sequence bounded below (or nondecreasing bounded above) converges by the Monotone Convergence Theorem, lim⁡sn\lim s_nlimsn​ and lim⁡in\lim i_nlimin​ are automatically well-defined whenever (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is bounded.

Basic Properties
  • lim inf⁡n→∞xn≤lim sup⁡n→∞xn\displaystyle \liminf_{n\to\infty} x_n \le \limsup_{n\to\infty} x_nn→∞liminf​xn​≤n→∞limsup​xn​.
  • lim inf⁡n→∞xn=−lim sup⁡n→∞(−xn)\displaystyle \liminf_{n\to\infty} x_n = -\limsup_{n\to\infty}(-x_n)n→∞liminf​xn​=−n→∞limsup​(−xn​).

Convergence Criterion via limsup/liminf

The real power of these two quantities is that they turn “does this sequence converge” into a simple equality check — no need to guess a candidate limit in advance.

Convergence Criterion

A sequence (xn)(x_n)(xn​) converges to L∈RL \in \mathbb{R}L∈R if and only if

lim inf⁡n→∞xn=lim sup⁡n→∞xn=L.\liminf_{n\to\infty} x_n = \limsup_{n\to\infty} x_n = L.n→∞liminf​xn​=n→∞limsup​xn​=L.
Proof

(⇒\Rightarrow⇒) Suppose xn→Lx_n \to Lxn​→L. For any ϵ>0\epsilon > 0ϵ>0, there exists NNN such that L−ϵ≤xn≤L+ϵL - \epsilon \le x_n \le L+\epsilonL−ϵ≤xn​≤L+ϵ for all n≥Nn \ge Nn≥N. Thus L+ϵL+\epsilonL+ϵ is an eventual upper bound and L−ϵL-\epsilonL−ϵ is an eventual lower bound, so

lim sup⁡xn≤L+ϵ,lim inf⁡xn≥L−ϵ.\limsup x_n \le L+\epsilon, \qquad \liminf x_n \ge L - \epsilon.limsupxn​≤L+ϵ,liminfxn​≥L−ϵ.

Letting ϵ↓0\epsilon \downarrow 0ϵ↓0 gives lim inf⁡xn=lim sup⁡xn=L\liminf x_n = \limsup x_n = Lliminfxn​=limsupxn​=L.

(⇐\Leftarrow⇐) Suppose lim inf⁡xn=lim sup⁡xn=L\liminf x_n = \limsup x_n = Lliminfxn​=limsupxn​=L but, toward a contradiction, xn↛Lx_n \nrightarrow Lxn​↛L. By the Characterizations of Non-Convergence (Lecture 9), there exists ϵ0>0\epsilon_0 > 0ϵ0​>0 and a subsequence (xnk)(x_{n_k})(xnk​​) with ∣xnk−L∣≥ϵ0|x_{n_k} - L| \ge \epsilon_0∣xnk​​−L∣≥ϵ0​ for all kkk. Then either infinitely many terms satisfy xnk≥L+ϵ0x_{n_k} \ge L+\epsilon_0xnk​​≥L+ϵ0​, or infinitely many satisfy xnk≤L−ϵ0x_{n_k} \le L - \epsilon_0xnk​​≤L−ϵ0​.

  • If infinitely many xnk≥L+ϵ0x_{n_k} \ge L+\epsilon_0xnk​​≥L+ϵ0​, then L+ϵ0L+\epsilon_0L+ϵ0​ fails to be an eventual upper bound, so lim sup⁡xn≥L+ϵ0>L\limsup x_n \ge L+\epsilon_0 > Llimsupxn​≥L+ϵ0​>L — contradicting lim sup⁡xn=L\limsup x_n = Llimsupxn​=L.
  • If infinitely many xnk≤L−ϵ0x_{n_k} \le L-\epsilon_0xnk​​≤L−ϵ0​, then L−ϵ0L-\epsilon_0L−ϵ0​ fails to be an eventual lower bound, so lim inf⁡xn≤L−ϵ0<L\liminf x_n \le L-\epsilon_0 < Lliminfxn​≤L−ϵ0​<L — contradicting lim inf⁡xn=L\liminf x_n = Lliminfxn​=L.

Either case is a contradiction, so xn→Lx_n \to Lxn​→L.

xn=(−1)nx_n = (-1)^nxn​=(−1)n

Here

lim sup⁡n→∞xn=1,lim inf⁡n→∞xn=−1,\limsup_{n\to\infty} x_n = 1, \qquad \liminf_{n\to\infty} x_n = -1,n→∞limsup​xn​=1,n→∞liminf​xn​=−1,

and since these disagree, the Convergence Criterion confirms what we already knew from Lecture 9 (via the two-subsequences divergence test): the sequence does not converge.

Useful equalities, restated

For any sequence (xn)(x_n)(xn​),

lim sup⁡n→∞xn=lim⁡n→∞(sup⁡k≥nxk),lim inf⁡n→∞xn=lim⁡n→∞(inf⁡k≥nxk).\limsup_{n\to\infty} x_n = \lim_{n\to\infty}\Bigl(\sup_{k\ge n} x_k\Bigr), \qquad \liminf_{n\to\infty} x_n = \lim_{n\to\infty}\Bigl(\inf_{k\ge n} x_k\Bigr).n→∞limsup​xn​=n→∞lim​(k≥nsup​xk​),n→∞liminf​xn​=n→∞lim​(k≥ninf​xk​).

This is just the Tail Sup/Inf Characterization above, worth keeping at hand as the go-to computational definition: rather than working with the sets VVV and WWW directly, compute the running tail sup/inf and take its limit — which, by the Monotone Convergence Theorem, is guaranteed to exist whenever the sequence is bounded.
